chunk_summary: A stepwise strategy for investigating growth faltering emphasizes confirming true faltering, assessing complications, and using targeted tests based on history, symptoms, and severity, while avoiding unnecessary broad testing.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for confirming true growth faltering using weight-for-length/height and BMI-for-age metrics.
Identify when to stabilize and refer children with complications such as dehydration or respiratory distress.
Learn to implement a behaviour-first trial for uncomplicated cases with behavioural feeding issues.
Recognize appropriate use of targeted diagnostic tests based on clinical presentation and suspected conditions.
key_facts: Growth faltering is confirmed by repeated measurements showing weight-for-length/height under the 5th percentile (under 5 years) or BMI-for-age under the 5th percentile (from 2 years).
Complicated cases (e.g., dehydration, persistent vomiting, lethargy) require stabilization and referral; counselling alone is unsafe.
A 2–4 week behaviour-first trial is recommended for uncomplicated cases with behavioural feeding issues.
Initial targeted tests include CBC, electrolytes, BUN/creatinine, urinalysis, and stool screening for gastrointestinal symptoms.
India-specific add-ons: ESR/CRP for chronic infection, TSH for poor linear growth, and celiac serology for gluten-related symptoms.
Microcytic anemia with high RDW suggests iron deficiency, while microcytosis with normal RDW points to thalassemia trait.
reference_quote: A safe investigation strategy for growth faltering balances missed disease against low-yield 'shotgun' panels. Use a graded pathway.
document_key_topics: Behavioral and dietary interventions for pediatric growth issues
Targeted diagnostic testing for suspected underlying conditions
